figures.caption: TABLE I: Results (nDCG@10) of reranking top-100 passages on the BEIR benchmark. Ret. denotes the first-stage retrieval model. The overall best result is in bold; the best within each block is underlined.
TABLE II: Results (nDCG@10) of reranking top-100 passages on TREC DL. Ret. denotes the first-stage retrieval model. Best overall in bold; best per block underlined.
Fig. 2: Effectiveness vs. efficiency trade-off on TREC DL19 and Covid. ResRank achieves the highest nDCG@10 while processing only 100 tokens (one per passage) and generating zero output tokens.
Fig. 3: Ablation study summary. BEIR Avg. and TREC DL Avg. (mean of DL19 and DL20) for the full model and each ablation variant. Every component contributes to the final performance.

Fig. 1: Architecture overview of ResRank. Each passage is independently compressed into a single embedding by the Encoder-LLM and directly fed into the Reranker-LLM alongside tokenized instruction and query. The reranker contextualizes all inputs through causal attention, producing hidden states at each passage position. A residual connection combines the original encoder embedding with the reranker’s contextualized hidden state to form the fused passage embedding. The global aggregation embedding at the [EOS] position captures the holistic ranking context, and final relevance scores are computed via cosine similarity between the global aggregation embedding and each fused passage embedding, entirely eliminating autoregressive decoding.
TABLE V: Ablation study results (nDCG@10). Each row removes one component from the full ResRank model.
